name: Gasteria
etymology: Greek gaster (stomach) — swollen flower base

name: Lithops
etymology: Greek lithos (stone) + ops (face) — stone-like appearance

name: Ferocactus
etymology: Latin ferox (fierce) + cactus — formidable spines

name: Stapelia
etymology: Named for Johannes van Stapel, 17th-century botanist

name: Echeveria
etymology: Named for Atanasio Echeverria, Mexican botanical illustrator

name: Helleborus
etymology: Greek helein (to injure) + bora (food) — poisonous yet medicinal

name: Aquilegia
etymology: Latin aquila (eagle) — petals resemble eagle talons

name: Heliotrope
etymology: Greek helios (sun) + tropos (turn) — turns toward the sun

name: Agave
etymology: Greek agauos (noble, admirable) — majestic form

name: Tillandsia
etymology: Named for Elias Tillands, Finnish botanist

name: Euphorbia
etymology: Named for Euphorbus, physician to King Juba II of Numidia

name: Nepenthes
etymology: Greek ne (not) + penthos (sorrow) — grief-banishing, mythical potion

name: Drosera
etymology: Greek droseros (dewy) — glistening dewdrop secretions

name: Digitalis
etymology: Latin digitus (finger) — thimble-shaped flowers

name: Delphinium
etymology: Greek delphin (dolphin) — flower bud resembles dolphin nose

name: Verbena
etymology: Latin verbena (sacred bough) — used in Roman religious rites

name: Pachypodium
etymology: Greek pachys (thick) + podion (foot) — thick-stemmed base

name: Senecio
etymology: Latin senex (old man) — gray-haired seedheads

name: Sedum
etymology: Latin sedere (to sit) — low-growing, sitting on rocks

name: Campanula
etymology: Italian campana (bell) — bell-shaped blossoms

name: Calceolaria
etymology: Latin calceolus (small shoe) — pouched slipper-shaped flowers

name: Ceratostigma
etymology: Greek keras (horn) + stigma (mark) — horn-shaped stigma

name: Penstemon
etymology: Greek pente (five) + stemon (thread) — five stamens, one sterile

name: Vulpes
etymology: Latin vulpes (fox) — cunning, adaptive predator

name: Corvus
etymology: Latin corvus (raven) — intelligence, myth, messenger

name: Octopus
etymology: Greek okto (eight) + pous (foot) — eight-armed

name: Morpho
etymology: Greek morphe (form, shape) — shapeshifting, structural color

name: Aurelia
etymology: Latin aureus (golden) — golden luminescence

name: Velella
etymology: Latin velum (sail) — sails the ocean surface

name: Physalia
etymology: Greek physallis (bubble) — gas-filled float

name: Hydra
etymology: Greek hydor (water) — mythical regenerating creature

name: Daphnia
etymology: Named for Daphne, nymph who transformed into a laurel tree

name: Chrysaora
etymology: Greek chrysos (gold) — golden bell and flowing tentacles

name: Attacus
etymology: Greek attakos (much-enduring) — the largest moths

name: Papilio
etymology: Latin papilio (butterfly) — the archetypal butterfly

name: Vanessa
etymology: Invented by Jonathan Swift for Esther Vanhomrigh

name: Strix
etymology: Greek strix (owl) — night vision, wisdom, myth

name: Falco
etymology: Latin falco (falcon, sickle) — curved talons, speed

name: Apis
etymology: Latin apis (bee) — industry, collective intelligence

name: Lucanus
etymology: Named for Lucanus, Roman region — impressive mandibles

name: Astrea
etymology: Greek astraios (starry) — star-shaped coral colonies

name: Lymantria
etymology: Greek lyma (destruction, ruin) — defoliating caterpillars

name: Buteo
etymology: Latin buteo (buzzard) — soaring raptor

name: Lupus
etymology: Latin lupus (wolf) — pack hunter, loyalty, wildness

name: Ursa
etymology: Latin ursa (bear) — strength, protection, constellation

name: Obsidian
etymology: Named for Obsius, Roman explorer who discovered it in Ethiopia

name: Labradorite
etymology: Discovered in Labrador, Canada — displays labradorescence

name: Beryl
etymology: Greek beryllos (sea-green stone) — includes emerald and aquamarine

name: Spinel
etymology: Latin spinella (little thorn) — pointed octahedral crystals

name: Zircon
etymology: Arabic zarqun (gold-colored) — oldest mineral on Earth

name: Onyx
etymology: Greek onyx (claw, fingernail) — layered bands of color

name: Jasper
etymology: Greek iaspis (spotted stone) — opaque, patterned quartz

name: Agate
etymology: Greek Achates, river in Sicily where first found — banded beauty

name: Garnet
etymology: Latin granatum (pomegranate) — red seed-like crystals

name: Topaz
etymology: Greek topazos — island in the Red Sea, or Sanskrit tapas (fire)

name: Malachite
etymology: Greek molokhe (mallow) — green like mallow leaves, banded

name: Azurite
etymology: Arabic lazaward (blue) via Persian lazhward — deep blue

name: Carnelian
etymology: Latin carnis (flesh) — warm flesh-colored translucent stone

name: Peridot
etymology: Arabic faridat (gem) — volcanic origin, golden-green

name: Mica
etymology: Latin micare (to glitter, flash) — perfectly cleaving sheets

name: Pyrite
etymology: Greek pyr (fire) — sparks when struck against steel

name: Serpentine
etymology: Latin serpens (snake) — mottled green, snake-skin pattern

name: Opal
etymology: Sanskrit upala (precious stone) — play of spectral colors

name: Fluorite
etymology: Latin fluere (to flow) — used as flux in smelting

name: Halite
etymology: Greek hals (salt) — cubic salt crystals

name: Tourmaline
etymology: Sinhalese turamali (stone of mixed colors) — piezoelectric

name: Amber
etymology: Arabic anbar (ambergris) — fossilized tree resin, preserves time

name: Calcite
etymology: Latin calx (lime) — double refraction, ubiquitous

name: Amanita
etymology: Named for Amanon, a mountain in Cilicia — iconic toadstool

name: Cortinarius
etymology: Latin cortina (curtain) — cobweb-like partial veil

name: Trametes
etymology: Latin trames (beam, path) — shelf-like growth on wood

name: Ganoderma
etymology: Greek ganos (brightness) + derma (skin) — lacquered surface

name: Morchella
etymology: German Morchel (morel) — honeycomb-patterned cap

name: Marasmius
etymology: Greek marasmos (withering) — can dry out and revive

name: Mycena
etymology: Greek mykes (mushroom, fungus) — small, delicate, bioluminescent

name: Boletus
etymology: Greek boles (clod, lump) — thick, meaty fruiting body

name: Xylaria
etymology: Greek xylon (wood) — grows on dead wood, dark club-shaped

name: Russula
etymology: Latin russulus (reddish) — vividly colored, brittle gills

name: Clavaria
etymology: Latin clava (club) — coral-like branched fruiting bodies

name: Nephila
etymology: Greek nema (thread) + philos (loving) — fond of spinning, golden silk webs

name: Trichonephila
etymology: Greek thrix (hair) + Nephila — hairy spinner of golden silk

name: Syngonium
etymology: Greek syn (together) + gone (seed/ovary) — united ovaries

name: Monstera
etymology: Latin monstrum (monster, portent, remarkable thing) — monstrous fenestrated leaves

name: Medinilla
etymology: Named for Jose de Medinilla y Pineda, Spanish governor of the Mariana Islands

name: Philodendron
etymology: Greek philos (loving) + dendron (tree) — tree-loving climber

name: Anthurium
etymology: Greek anthos (flower) + oura (tail) — tail-like spadix

name: Microsorum
etymology: Greek mikros (small) + soros (heap) — small spore heaps on fronds

name: Dieffenbachia
etymology: Named for Joseph Dieffenbach, head gardener at Schonbrunn Palace, Vienna

name: Aglaonema
etymology: Greek aglaos (bright, shining) + nema (thread) — bright thread, showy stamens

name: Aspidistra
etymology: Greek aspidion (small shield) — shield-shaped stigma

name: Rhipsalis
etymology: Greek rhips (wickerwork) — entangled, intertwined branches

name: Scindapsus
etymology: Greek skindapsos — an Indian musical instrument used to tame wild elephants

name: Coffea
etymology: Arabic qahwa (wine, dark stuff) — the dark, stimulating brew

name: Theobroma
etymology: Greek theos (god) + broma (food) — food of the gods

name: Sansevieria
etymology: Named for Raimondo di Sangro, Prince of San Severo — Italian nobleman and inventor

name: Fugue
etymology: Latin fuga (flight, fleeing) — interweaving voices in musical pursuit

name: Isomorphism
etymology: Greek isos (equal) + morphe (form) — structural equivalence between systems

name: Mu
etymology: Japanese mu from Chinese wu — nothingness, unasking the question

name: Mobius
etymology: Named for August Mobius, German mathematician — one-sided surface

name: Recursion
etymology: Latin recursio (running back, return) — self-referential application

name: Theorem
etymology: Greek theorema (spectacle, sight) — a proposition proved from axioms

name: Holos
etymology: Greek holos (whole, entire) — the irreducible whole

name: Canon
etymology: Greek kanon (rule, straight rod) — strict rule-bound musical composition

name: Nexus
etymology: Latin nexus (binding together) — a connection point

name: Quintessence
etymology: Latin quinta essentia (fifth essence) — the element above earth/air/fire/water

name: Monolith
etymology: Greek monos (alone) + lithos (stone) — single stone, cosmic sentinel

name: Nebula
etymology: Latin nebula (mist, fog, cloud) — cosmic cradle of stars

name: Tesseract
etymology: Greek tessera (four) + aktis (ray) — four-dimensional hypercube

name: Sanctum
etymology: Latin sanctum (sacred place, shrine) — consecrated protected space

name: Astral
etymology: Latin astralis (of the stars) — ethereal star-body projection

name: Mandala
etymology: Sanskrit mandala — container of essence, sacred cosmic diagram

name: Heptapod
etymology: Greek hepta (seven) + pous (foot) — seven-limbed, non-linear time beings

name: Logogram
etymology: Greek logos (word) + gramma (that which is drawn) — word-sign

name: Shimmer
etymology: Old English scimerian (to shine brightly) — refracted mutation zone

name: Replicant
etymology: Latin replicare (to fold back) — bioengineered copy, identity crisis

name: Synecdoche
etymology: Greek syn (together) + ek (out) + dechesthai (receive) — part standing for whole

name: Totem
etymology: Algonquian (Ojibwe) ototeman (kinship-group) — reality anchor, personal proof

name: Paradox
etymology: Greek paradoxon (contrary to expectation) — impossible yet true

name: Prestige
etymology: Latin praestigium (delusion, illusion) — the climactic reveal in magic

name: Prometheus
etymology: Greek pro (before) + manthano (learn) — the forethinker who stole fire

name: Trinity
etymology: Latin trinitas (three-ness) — divine power, atomic genesis

name: Sicario
etymology: Latin sicarius from sica (curved dagger) — the dagger-man, hired assassin

name: Glitch
etymology: Yiddish glitsh (slippery place) — dimensional instability, reality flicker

name: Multiverse
etymology: Latin multus (many) + universus (all turned together) — parallel dimensions

name: Futura
etymology: Latin futura (things to come) — geometric sans-serif, typographic modernism

name: Diorama
etymology: Greek dio (through) + orama (that which is seen) — miniature contained world

name: Midsommar
etymology: Swedish midsommar (midsummer) — endless daylight, pagan ritual, cyclical rebirth

name: Aureliano
etymology: Latin aureus (golden) from aurum (gold) — golden lineage, cyclical repetition

name: Cochlea
etymology: Latin cochlea (snail shell, spiral) — the spiral organ of hearing

name: Xibalba
etymology: K'iche' Maya — place of fright, the underworld of death and rebirth

name: Liminal
etymology: Latin limen (threshold) — the state of being between, on the edge

name: Melange
etymology: French melange (mixture) from mesler (to mix) — consciousness-expanding substance
